title: Daniel 11
subtitle: The Kings of the North and South

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Vision of Four Beasts

heb.paragraph: The most detailed predictive prophecy in the Bible. The angel traces kings from Persia (v.2: three more kings then Xerxes), through Alexander (v.3: divided to the four winds), to the Ptolemies (south) and Seleucids (north) warring over Israel for two centuries. Every verse corresponds to documented history.

refs.note: The ram and goat vision — the same empires described with different imagery.
The bronze and iron kingdoms of the statue — Greece and its successors.
The Maccabean narrative that documents the events Daniel 11 prophesies.

notes.note: Three more Persian kings, then a mighty king (Alexander) whose empire splits. Same vision as ch.8 with historical detail.
The Ptolemaic-Seleucid wars. Verse-by-verse history of 3rd–2nd century BC. The "beautiful land" = Israel, caught between empires.
"His successor will send out a tax collector. In a few years, destroyed — not in anger or battle." Seleucus IV and Heliodorus.

Calvin traces each verse to its historical fulfilment, marvelling at the precision. He uses the chapter as evidence for the divine inspiration of Scripture: no human author in the 6th century could have predicted 3rd–2nd century politics with this accuracy.
Calvin notes the "beautiful land" (Israel) is the stage on which empires perform. Israel’s geography — the land bridge between Africa and Asia — makes it the eternal battleground of empires.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET identifies the four Persian kings as Cambyses, pseudo-Smerdis, Darius I, and Xerxes I. The "fourth" whose wealth provokes conflict with Greece is Xerxes, whose invasion of Greece (480 BC) set the stage for Alexander’s retaliatory conquest.
The NET provides the most detailed verse-by-verse historical identifications, noting that scholars achieve nearly complete agreement on the Ptolemaic-Seleucid correspondences.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the chapter’s historical precision is either the strongest evidence for supernatural prophecy or the strongest evidence for late dating. There is no neutral position. The reader’s prior commitments determine which conclusion is drawn.
Longman notes the moral pattern beneath the political detail: every king’s rise is temporary, every alliance is broken, every victory is reversed. The political lesson: no human arrangement endures.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay reads the chapter as "interpreted history" — political events given theological significance. The details are not trivia but evidence: God governs the specific movements of empires, not just their general trajectory.
Goldingay: the "beautiful land" stands at the intersection of every geopolitical conflict. Israel’s centrality is not accidental but providential: the land of promise is also the land of testing.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins notes this is the passage that most sharply divides conservative and critical scholars on Daniel’s date. Critical scholars argue no 6th-century author could produce this precision; the prophecy must have been written after the events (vaticinium ex eventu). Conservative scholars counter that divine inspiration explains the accuracy.
Collins identifies "the beautiful land" (eret-hatstsevi) as Israel, the same term as 8:9. The land’s beauty is theological, not merely geographical: it is the place of God’s dwelling.

hist.context: This chapter contains more historically verifiable predictions than any other passage in the Bible. The Ptolemaic-Seleucid wars (vv.5–20) can be mapped verse by verse onto the historical record from 323 to 175 BC. The "beautiful land" (v.16) is Israel, caught between the two rival Hellenistic kingdoms. The marriage alliance of v.6 is the union of Berenice (Ptolemy II’s daughter) and Antiochus II (c.250 BC). The invasion of v.7–8 is Ptolemy III’s retaliatory campaign.

heb.paragraph: "They will set up the abomination (shiqquts) that causes desolation" (v.31). Antiochus IV Epiphanes: the "contemptible person" (v.21) who seizes power through intrigue. He invades Egypt, is humiliated by Rome (v.30, "ships of Kittim"), takes out his rage on Jerusalem. He abolishes the daily sacrifice and sets up the abomination — an altar to Zeus in the temple. "Those who know their God will firmly resist" (v.32). Verses 36–45 may shift to a future antichrist: "the king who exalts himself above every god" (v.36).

refs.note: Jesus: "When you see the abomination of desolation spoken through Daniel" — applying this text to the future.
The man of lawlessness who sets himself up in God’s temple — developing Daniel 11:36.
The historical account: they set up the abomination of desolation on the altar and built altars in the surrounding towns.
The beast who speaks blasphemies and is given authority over every tribe — developing the horn/king of Daniel 11.

notes.note: "They will set up the abomination that causes desolation." Jesus quotes this in Matt 24:15 as a sign of the end.
"The people who know their God will firmly resist. Those who are wise will instruct many." Knowing God produces resistance; wisdom produces teaching under persecution.
"Some of the wise will stumble, so that they may be refined." Suffering as purification. The refining has a terminus: "the time of the end."
"The king will exalt himself above every god." At this point, many see the text shifting to a future figure. Self-exaltation "above every god" exceeds Antiochus and echoes 2 Thess 2:4.

Calvin identifies the abomination as Antiochus’s altar to Zeus in the temple (167 BC). He then notes Jesus’ reapplication to a future event, suggesting the pattern of temple-desecration recurs across history.
Calvin takes special encouragement from "those who know their God will firmly resist." He applies this directly to Protestants facing persecution: knowledge of God produces courage under tyranny.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET provides the historical detail: Antiochus sent his general Apollonius to Jerusalem, built a fortress (Akra), and erected an altar to Zeus Olympios on the temple’s great altar on December 25, 167 BC.

The NET notes the scholarly debate on whether these verses describe Antiochus (continuing the sequence) or a future figure (breaking the historical sequence). The language of v.36 ("exalts himself above every god") seems to exceed what any historical figure achieved.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the "abomination of desolation" creates a prophetic chain: Daniel prophesies it → Antiochus fulfils it → Jesus reapplies it → the NT expects a final fulfilment. Each iteration amplifies the pattern.

Longman: "those who know their God" is the chapter’s pastoral centre. In the midst of the most detailed political prophecy in the Bible, the essential message is personal: know God, and you will stand firm.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the "abomination" (shiqquts) is a contemptuous pun on the Phoenician deity Baal Shamem (“Lord of Heaven”), repointed as shiqquts shomem (“abomination of desolation”). The wordplay is deliberate theological mockery.
Goldingay reads the transition at v.36 as the text’s eschatological horizon expanding: the historical Antiochus becomes a type of the ultimate opponent of God. The specific becomes paradigmatic.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins provides detailed verse-by-verse historical identifications through v.39. He notes that vv.40–45 do not correspond to any known event in Antiochus’s career — suggesting either unfulfilled prophecy or a shift in referent.

Collins reads the "wise" (maskilim) as the forerunners of the apocalyptic community, possibly proto-Essenes. Their response to persecution is not military (unlike the Maccabees) but instructional and martyrological.

hist.context: Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175–164 BC) is the "contemptible person" of v.21. His desecration of the Jerusalem temple in December 167 BC is the historical event behind "the abomination of desolation" (v.31). Jesus references this in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:15), applying it to a future event (possibly the Roman destruction of AD 70 or an end-times desecration). The transition at v.36 is debated: many scholars see the description exceeding Antiochus and pointing to a future eschatological figure.

ppl.text: "The people who know their God will firmly resist…

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: 16) is Israel, caught between the two rival Hellenistic kingdoms…

ppl.name: son
ppl.text: Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175–164 BC) is the "contemptible person" of v…

ppl.text: Those who are wise will instruct many…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAfter some years, they will become allies. The daughter of the king of the South will go to the king of the North to make an alliance, but she will not retain her power, and he and his power will not last. In those days she will be betrayed, together with her royal escort and her father and the one who supported her.ESVAfter some years they shall make an alliance, and the daughter of the king of the south shall come to the king of the north to make an agreement. But she shall not retain the strength of her arm, and he and his arm shall not endure, but she shall be given up, and her attendants, he who fathered her, and he who supported her in those times.NIVAfter he has arisen, his empire will be broken up and parceled out toward the four winds of heaven. It will not go to his descendants, nor will it have the power he exercised, because his empire will be uprooted and given to others.ESVAnd as soon as he has arisen, his kingdom shall be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven, but not to his posterity, nor according to the authority with which he ruled, for his kingdom shall be plucked up and go to others besides these.

src.title: The Prayer of Nabonidus (4Q242, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A fragmentary Qumran text describes a Babylonian king struck with illness for seven years who is healed by a Jewish exorcist — strikingly parallel to Nebuchadnezzar’s madness in Daniel 4.
src.note: The Prayer of Nabonidus may preserve an older version of the tradition behind Daniel 4, or reflect a common Near Eastern motif of royal humbling. Either way, it confirms the antiquity of the Daniel traditions.

src.title: 1 Maccabees 1–2 (2nd c. BC)
src.quote: Describes the historical desecration of the Jerusalem temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC — the event Daniel 8:9–14 and 11:31 prophesy as the “abomination of desolation.”
src.note: The fulfilment is documented in non-biblical Jewish sources, confirming Daniel’s prophetic precision whether the prophecy is dated to the 6th or 2nd century BC.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 10.11 (1st c. AD)
src.quote: Josephus records that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem (332 BC), the high priest showed him Daniel’s prophecy about a Greek king conquering Persia, and Alexander was so impressed he offered sacrifices at the temple.
src.note: Whether historically accurate or legendary, Josephus’s account shows that Daniel’s prophecies were understood as referring to Greek conquest well before the Christian era.

rec.text: "Three more Persian kings, then a mighty king (Alexander) whose empire splits. Same vision as ch.8 with historical detail."

rows.label: Historical Sweep — 11:1–20
rows.text: Persia → Alexander → Ptolemies vs Seleucids. Verse-by-verse documented history.

hebtext.note: The most detailed predictive prophecy in the Bible. The angel traces kings from Persia (v.2: three more kings then Xerxes), through Alexander (v.3: divided to the four winds), to the Ptolemies (south) and Seleucids (north) warring over Israel for two centuries. Every verse corresponds to documented history.

"They will set up the abomination (shiqquts) that causes desolation" (v.31). Antiochus IV Epiphanes: the "contemptible person" (v.21) who seizes power through intrigue. He invades Egypt, is humiliated by Rome (v.30, "ships of Kittim"), takes out his rage on Jerusalem. He abolishes the daily sacrifice and sets up the abomination — an altar to Zeus in the temple. "Those who know their God will firmly resist" (v.32). Verses 36–45 may shift to a future antichrist: "the king who exalts himself above every god" (v.36).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the most detailed predictive prophecy in the Bible. Jesus quotes v.31 (Matt 24:15). Judgment: Antiochus as divine instrument and future antichrist type.

coaching.tip: The prophecy traces the Ptolemaic-Seleucid wars with such precision — 'the king of the South' and 'the king of the North' trading victories, marriages, and betrayals — that it reads like history written after the fact. For those who accept predictive prophecy, it demonstrates God's detailed knowledge of the future. For skeptics, it dates the book. Either way, the text refuses to be ignored: it forces a decision about the nature of prophetic revelation.

'He will set up the abomination that causes desolation' (v. 31). Antiochus IV placed a pagan altar in the Jerusalem temple in 167 BC — the historical trigger for the Maccabean revolt. Jesus reuses the phrase in Mark 13:14 for a future event, suggesting the pattern repeats. Daniel's 'abomination' becomes a recurring category: the desecration of sacred space by hostile power. Each fulfillment points to the next until the pattern reaches its final expression.

prayer_prompt: As the detailed prophecy of warring kings unfolds with precision, consider asking God whether you believe He is that specific about history — and that attentive to the details of your own life.
